region_of_origin: China (Fujian and Guangdong provinces); widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of brewed oolong tea (approximately 240 ml / 240 g) is the typical single serving consumed at breakfast or teatime in Singapore and across Chinese communities.

nutrient_source.title: Oolong Tea (brewed) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for plain brewed oolong tea per 100 g. All core macros and micronutrients are as reported; no adaptation was required.

gi_source.title: N/A — Oolong tea contains negligible carbohydrates (0.15 g/100 g); glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed oolong tea contains only 0.15 g carbohydrate per 100 g with 0 g sugar. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign a GI value to plain unsweetened tea because the carbohydrate content is too low to elicit a measurable glycaemic response. GI is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate beverages. Glycemic load per 240 ml serving is effectively 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (3 mg/100 g) and zero sugar; polyphenols support endothelial function
hypertension.guidance: Plain brewed oolong tea is appropriate for individuals with hypertension; its very low sodium content (3 mg/100 g) and absence of added sugar pose no blood-pressure risk, and regular consumption of tea polyphenols has been associated with modest reductions in systolic blood pressure in AHA-reviewed evidence — provided no sugar, sweetened creamers, or condensed milk are added.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero sugar and negligible carbohydrates (0.15 g/100 g); polyphenols associated with improved insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain oolong tea is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes: it contains no sugar and only 0.15 g carbohydrate per 100 g, producing no measurable glycaemic response, and its polyphenol content (catechins, theaflavins) has been associated with improved insulin sensitivity in ADA-aligned studies — consume plain without added sugar or sweetened milk to preserve this benefit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and cardioprotective polyphenols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain oolong tea is appropriate for individuals with cardiovascular disease: it contains no saturated fat, no cholesterol, and negligible sodium, while its polyphenol content has been associated with improved lipid profiles and reduced LDL oxidation in peer-reviewed cardiovascular research — avoid adding sugar, full-fat condensed milk, or creamers that would introduce saturated fat and excess calories.

rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Plain oolong tea may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its polyphenol content, which has documented anti-inflammatory properties relevant to RA management per EULAR dietary guidance — consume plain without milk or sugar to preserve the anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oolong tea (乌龙茶 / 烏龍茶, wū lóng chá) is a traditional Chinese tea that is partially oxidised, placing it between green tea (unoxidised) and black tea (fully oxidised) on the oxidation spectrum, typically ranging from 15% to 85% oxidation depending on the variety and processing style. It is brewed by steeping dried and partially fermented oolong tea leaves in hot water (typically 85–95°C) for 2–5 minutes. The resulting infusion is a pale golden to amber liquid with a complex flavour profile that can range from floral and fruity (lightly oxidised varieties such as Tie Guan Yin) to roasted and earthy (heavily oxidised varieties such as Da Hong Pao). Oolong tea is virtually calorie-free (1 kcal/100 g), contains no fat, no protein, and only 0.15 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it nutritionally negligible as a macronutrient source. It is naturally free of sugar and cholesterol. Its primary bioactive constituents are polyphenols — including catechins, theaflavins, and thearubigins — which have been associated with antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and metabolic benefits in peer-reviewed research. Oolong tea is widely consumed in Singapore, China, Taiwan, and across Chinese diaspora communities, commonly served at breakfast and teatime. It is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease when consumed plain without added sugar or sweetened milk. It is vegan, vegetarian, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is oolong tea good for diabetes?
How many calories in oolong tea?
Is oolong tea suitable for high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of oolong tea?
Oolong tea vs green tea vs black tea
Can I drink oolong tea if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. The food is a well-defined, standardised beverage (plain brewed oolong tea) with an exact match in the HPB database. All values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference data for brewed oolong tea (USDA SR Legacy item 14366), confirming cross-source consistency.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero: plain brewed oolong tea is an aqueous infusion of tea leaves and contains no protein in the liquid phase as reported by HPB Foodo and confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (item 14366). fat_g is naturally zero: plain brewed oolong tea contains no fat; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central. sugar_g is naturally zero: no sugars are present in plain unsweetened brewed oolong tea per HPB Foodo. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero: tea infusion contains no dietary fibre in the liquid phase. iron_mg is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo (0.0 mg/100 g). cholesterol_mg is naturally zero: plant-derived beverage contains no cholesterol. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid, individual vitamin, and moisture data for plain brewed oolong tea are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database entry used as the primary source; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy item 14366 reports water_g as approximately 99.84 g/100 g but this value is not present in the HPB source record and has not been substituted to maintain source fidelity. These fields are set to -1 to indicate data not available in the primary source, consistent with G3 numeric hygiene rules. Glycemic index is not applicable (set to null) because oolong tea contains only 0.15 g carbohydrate per 100 g, which is insufficient to produce a measurable glycaemic response; gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate beverages. Glycemic load per 240 ml serving is 0.0. The input record listed the condition key as 'diabetes'; this has been corrected to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301103 is a defensible match for oolong tea as a food entity; FoodEx2 code A00LC is used for oolong tea infusion; SNOMED CT code 227313005 is cited as a defensible match — reviewers should verify these codes against current ontology releases before production import.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Oolong Tea' is precise and unambiguous. Multilingual identity satisfied: Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi all present, satisfying G6 for Chinese-cuisine food consumed in Singapore.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (1.0), protein_g (0.0), fat_g (0.0), and carbohydrate_g (0.15) are all present and numeric. Zero values for protein_g and fat_g are justified in notes as naturally zero per HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy item 14366. Nutrient source is HPB Foodo, an approved primary source.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0), sodium_mg (3.0), saturated_fat_g (0.0), and sugar_g (0.0) are all present as numeric values, plausible, and supported by HPB Foodo.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is null (not_applicable for negligible-carbohydrate beverage per Veritas protocol — not set to 0 which would fail q4). gi_category is 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. gi_source is documented referencing University of Sydney GI Database with explanation that no GI is assigned due to negligible carbohydrate content.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301103 (oolong tea), FoodEx2 code A00LC (oolong tea infusion), and SNOMED CT code 227313005 (oolong tea substance) are all present and defensible. No codes are fabricated; gap note included advising reviewer verification.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters and covers: what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutritional profile. Common user queries and meal context are populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (low concern), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern, human_condition_name correctly set), Cardiovascular Disease (low concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Additional condition rheumatoid_arthritis preserved from input.

All values in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Coverage is comprehensive for a Chinese-origin beverage consumed in Singapore.
